edges.description: Paternal-genome excess and absent maternal allele dysregulate imprinted gene dosage, silencing maternally expressed CDKN1C/p57.
edges.source: Aberrant Fertilization and Parental Genome Imbalance
edges.target: Loss of Genomic Imprinting and p57 Silencing

edges.description: Loss of imprinting-dependent growth control drives excessive trophoblast proliferation and hydropic villous swelling.
edges.source: Loss of Genomic Imprinting and p57 Silencing

edges.description: Residual or persistent hyperproliferative trophoblast after evacuation can invade and progress to gestational trophoblastic neoplasia.

nodes.description: Hydatidiform mole originates from abnormal fertilization producing an excess of paternal relative to maternal genome. Complete moles are usually diploid androgenetic, arising when an ovum that has lost or inactivated its nucleus is fertilized and the paternal genome is duplicated (or by dispermy); partial moles are usually triploid diandric, arising when a normal ovum is fertilized by two sperm. This parental genome imbalance is the initiating event of molar pregnancy.
nodes.id: Aberrant Fertilization and Parental Genome Imbalance

evidence.snippet:
Hydatidiform moles are 
characterized by abnormal fertilization, resulting in villous hydrops and 
trophoblastic hyperplasia

being completely androgenetic in complete 
hydatidiform moles and diandric triploid in partial hydatidiform moles

nodes.description: The androgenetic/diandric genome imbalance dysregulates parent-of-origin imprinted genes. The maternally expressed, paternally imprinted gene CDKN1C (encoding the cyclin-dependent kinase inhibitor p57KIP2) is silenced when the maternal allele is absent (complete mole) or epigenetically disrupted (familial biparental mole), removing a brake on trophoblast proliferation. Negative p57 immunostaining of villous cytotrophoblast and stroma is the diagnostic hallmark of complete mole.
nodes.id: Loss of Genomic Imprinting and p57 Silencing

evidence.snippet:
fundamental genetic event leading to overall alteration of imprinting gene 
expression in the molar trophoblast

evidence.reference_title: Refined diagnosis of hydatidiform moles with p57 immunohistochemistry and molecular genotyping: updated analysis of a prospective series of 2217 cases.
evidence.snippet:
Of 564 
successfully immunostained CHMs, 563 (99.8%) were p57-negative

nodes.description: After uterine evacuation, a fraction of molar pregnancies progress to gestational trophoblastic neoplasia (persistent/invasive mole or choriocarcinoma), detected by a plateauing or rising hCG. The risk is substantially higher for complete than partial moles. This malignant sequela is captured in the separate Gestational Trophoblastic Neoplasm entry; here it is the principal downstream complication of molar pregnancy that mandates post-evacuation hCG surveillance.

evidence.snippet:
Posttreatment, regular 
β-hCG monitoring is essential to detect potential progression to gestational 
trophoblastic neoplasia (GTN).

the premalignant disorders of complete and partial 
hydatidiform mole, and the malignant disorders of invasive mole

nodes.description: Disinhibited trophoblast proliferation produces the defining pathology of molar pregnancy: enlarged, edematous (hydropic) chorionic villi with hyperplastic villous trophoblast. Excess syncytiotrophoblast secretes markedly elevated human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG), driving the clinical features (hyperemesis, large-for-dates uterus, theca-lutein cysts, and occasionally hyperthyroidism). This molar tissue constitutes the abnormal "grape-like" placental mass.
